text:
I think the real scissor here is between European and North American cultural norms. 

A while ago I left my jacket in my friends room in a European hotel. I had no connection to the room otherwise and my friend was busy. I walked to the front desk, asked for a room key, was given it and got my jacket. No questions asked anywhere. 

In NA all the norms pertaining to rules are a lot more absolute. I was once nearly thrown out of a games convention because I wasn't wearing my badge around my neck and only presenting it to the guards at the entrance because "the rules". To the European brain this is inconveivable because there's some sense in which it's common sense that the badge is for getting past security so even if the rules say "you have to wear it" on paper everybody would be fine with accomplishing the functional understanding. 

Same here, to most north Americans there is a big deontological category "lying" or "stealing" in this squarely fits the definition. 

To me very obviously, taking an item somebody else doesn't want isn't stealing, the category of stealing is about making sure people's property isn't taken away. This reads closer to dumpster diving than taking an item from the supermarket to me very obviously. BUT in there is an explicit step to switch to second order reasoning that uses something like intent instead of categorical enforcement of norms - which requires incidentally a different kind of high social trust about the intent of the people involved. In this particular kind of social trust Europe is a higher trust society than North America. 

I'm guessing most of this pretty straightforwardly falls out from historical contigency, where the US had to operate at a very different scales, so more explicit/simpler norms are needed (or reddotairplane.gif). Also I think an important reason why reasnoning about e.g. Scandinavian countries doesn't just translate the US 1 to 1.

Also often this is hardcoded into the system in a way. Where e.g. another place where I've encountered this before: I wear contacts and ran out while in the US. I tried to buy some. They told me I needed a US prescription or I could go fuck myself because those are the rules. I was thoroughly confused why nobody would sell me this harmless object I clearly know I need, why the employees can't just sell me that, when a friend pointed out that they'd potentially be fired for this under threat of the legal system. 

Both sides look like barbarians to the other because they're violating their own versions of high trust, but it's not actually a low trust phenomenon but slightly different flavours of high trust clashing.

Hi, appreciate this. I think Maeby's characterisation sounds roughly right about what I'm trying to communicate.

The argument is the high trust is in the intent of people, so you have to do trust-breaking behaviours on that level for it to be (perceived as) damaging. 

There is also an implicit assumption that it's low harm bc most low-value things like that get left in lost and found and it's extremely unlikely that you'd pick the scarf of exactly the one person who does otherwise.

text:
Went to vibecamp 2 in 2023, knowing 0 people personally, having never posted on twitter, a complete lurker. It was a transcendent experience, first time in my adult life that I truly didn't feel alone in that there were other people with similar experiences to me. Changed my life trajectory more than most other events (I literally live on a different continent because of second order connections from it!). If you're at all considering going, especially if you feel like you might not belong because you're a lurker, just go and hang out, it will almost certainly be wonderful!

PS: If you do do this for the first time, I recommend taking 3 days off afterwards to integrate instead of trying to go straight back to live, it will fuck you up.

text: I remember Tom Bilyeu on Attia saying (paraphrase) "ohh I starting liking meditation once I had my rat explanation for it that it puts you into the autonomous nervous system" or sth like that and that always stuck with me (2/x)

raw.full_text: I remember Tom Bilyeu on Attia saying (paraphrase) "ohh I starting liking meditation once I had my rat explanation for it that it puts you into the autonomous nervous system" or sth like that and that always stuck with me (2/x)

text: So breathing is a quintessential atonomous nervous system thing and it always confused me a bit why the fuck we can control it so much? Like here's this process that if you turn it off humans just die and... well do what you want with it. Why would evolution do this? (3/x)

raw.full_text: So breathing is a quintessential atonomous nervous system thing and it always confused me a bit why the fuck we can control it so much? Like here's this process that if you turn it off humans just die and... well do what you want with it. Why would evolution do this? (3/x)

text: But then OFC it's not at all just "do what you want" but it's this weird process where it kind of runs in the background except sometimes it doesn't! and you can tap into it and override it and then notably I used to get stuck in weird ways "leaving" control over my breath (4/x)

raw.full_text: But then OFC it's not at all just "do what you want" but it's this weird process where it kind of runs in the background except sometimes it doesn't! and you can tap into it and override it and then notably I used to get stuck in weird ways "leaving" control over my breath (4/x)

text: This I believe is exactly analogous to how other tension gets stored and interacted with in the body where you can "take control" of a muscle by paying careful attention to it with your other nervous system and make it do exactly what you want to learn a new pattern (5/x)

text:
at least in these parts it's *the* most language + subjective psychology fucked topic imaginable. people mean like 734283240 different things at the same time, do a version of smuggling to justify whatever ethical position they actually ambiently absorbed from liberalism with philosophy with it and so on.

here's a non-exhaustive list of different things it refers to:
- "having qualia" - (IMO fake idea that doesn't even really make sense)
- existence itself or the fact that STH exists rather than nothing or something like that - (IMO the correct "base axiom" to have is intelligibility)
- self modelling capacities of living beings at various levels 
- sapience (the specific level of abstraciton/reflection that humans are uniquely supercharged at) => this is the one yudkowsky cares about
- moral patienthood

then there's this big huge giant issue that people use conciousness as a justification for their ego => if you imply to them that some of these concepts are weird and fake and sollipsistic their egoic defenses come online and you're fighting those at the same time.

there's like this famous anecdote my psychiatrist told me that if you're perfectly rational in modelling the world and yourself you become depressed => there's some very fundamental bias going on in people that they *need to feel special* in a way to function in the first place as humans and they conflate this with the idea of conciousness (or more precisely that's the kind of jesus smuggling they engage in) 

that's probably I?m guessing what you viscerally experience once you're at this thisdell level you referred to in your post (cf picture)

(>type of guy needs a full cessation to realise the world keeps running outside of him)

so anyway I writing is hard this is vague I don't know how to put this into words well but this is what endless hours of sam harris and sean carrol podcasts has done to my schizoid mind

there's like this famous anecdote my psychiatrist told me that if you're perfectly rational in modelling the world and yourself you become depressed => there's some very fundamental bias going on in people that they *need to feel special* in a way to function in the first place as humans and they conflate this with the idea of conciousness (or more precisely that's the kind of jesus smuggling they engage in) 

that's probably I?m guessing what you viscerally experience once you're at this thisdell level you referred to in your post (cf picture)

(>type of guy needs a full cessation to realise the world keeps running outside of him)

so anyway I writing is hard this is vague I don't know how to put this into words well but this is what endless hours of sam harris and sean carrol podcasts has done to my schizoid mind

text:
I think what you're really not factoring in here is that certain types of mental illnesses *create* the conditions "on purpose" (subconciously as a strategy mostly though there's versions that are concious too!) => they will not react to being excluded with "learning from it" but instead by creating more drama and drawing people into it! Yes those people are "vulnerable" in some technical sense but also they are abusing this status! (implicitly mostly).

I've seen some of my most honest and well meaning friends suffer for literally *years* because they wouldn't pull the trigger on excluding "vulnerable" people who kept ruining situations out of some idea of wanting to help them => this is a defense against that, not against the slightly socially awkward person who's honest and well meaning and would learn from it. => those will mostly learn anyway and end up somewhere different where they are accepted!

text:
I've unfortunately been confronted with this phenomenon very directly (The person in question even used almost your exact quoted expression here to talk about a situation in their life). My read on the situation I saw up close is that they were on some level (too?) deeply aware that they're flawed => they do a ton of "work" in one area which really is a soothing strategy and I fact makes it easier for them to be shitty in other areas because they now have a credible story to tell themselves about how they've done the work so they're not the problem. 

And basically the more validation from external sources (Instagram) they get the easier it becomes for them to believe that they truly are better and don't have to put in the work in that other area that's so uncomfortable to look at.

That being said I sympathise, I do some of this myself and I think most people are guilty of this to some degree, it's just that social media hypercharges this phenomenon + selects for the worst cases.

text: to me it's the way more extraordinary claim. humans are *not* generally intelligent in that they're just good at any task. we can retrain to do tasks to some extent but have to specialise. there's like a fundamental discontinuity between physical things in the real world that causes this (cf reality is surprisingly full of detail blogpost). the only people regularly dismissing this kind of complexity are all disembodied head people spending a lot of their experiental time in extremely simplified environments (computers). yes, this people are smart, but also they are a very specific type of guy and limited by that! I've never encountered anybody not like that arguing for it

text:
I do agree that at some point that is inevitable, I disagree that the current approach is anywhere close. Especially bc humans + chimps are very similar in that they have natural biological survival/conquest based objective functions and ai have very different ones. Again at some point we're going to produce something organic like enough that it is going to be the scenario that you outline, I don't think we're there.

I also just bite the bullet on this not being a bad thing. I see it more as speciation. As a thought experiment, if you were a homo erectus warning about homo sapiens coming and making you extinct, knowing everything that happened after, would you mind? This is extremely related to the question of "are you fine ceding the future to your children?" which already people are (revealed preference at least) very against these days, which I do find abhorrent itself.

Also I do think likely some sort of "zoo scenario" would happen, basically the higher intelligence seems to come with longer time horizons and less very narrow zero sum competition. Again, humans also didn't kill the chimps or everyone in middle america. And also as much as the conquest was terrible, I also like what is there today more than what came before. I think a lot of the worldview also kind of hinges on making any assertion like that being absolutely completely taboo for political-ideological reasons in the circles that all of this is taking place.

text:
yeah I think we pretty much agree here.

imo a lot of spirituality is about surrender, especially to the future (kind of obviously what faith is I guess). 

as you highlighted ofc, even when recognising all of this there still is (and imo importantly should be!) a strong preference towards loved ones and good in immediate surroundings

then also I just personally still suck at this lol where I've in theory accepted it my revealed preference is I've not managed to get viscerally into a state of faith/surrender in many different ways. imo that's kind of the underlying shadow motivation of a lot of alignment (and also anti-aging research) basically a denial of the acceptance of death which is the same as a lack of surrender (which is not to say that I think the things are necessarily bad, I'm still pro anti-aging but I think being viscerally for it for unexamined shadow reasons of rejection of a future that's non-self is bad). 

also agreed on the darkness in the worthy successor crowd, I think a lot of those people are necessarily kind of dark because they're stuck in the stage of rejecting the other side out of habit (which is an antisocial tendency that ofc I also partially inhabit and in the past have been extremely guilty off)

ok so, I think there would be ways to reach him, but probably not arguments. this highlights a big issue I have with all of these kinds of arguments that they're kind of fundamentally disembodied. like "there's a set of words you can say" well e.g. your body language matters almost more than words when interacting with most normal people and so on

=> I think a lot of "not orthogonality" is based on like convergence of bodies to act in the physical world or stomething like that. even convergence of minds. e.g. LLMs have all of these weird ways they're failing that computers don't have, that are actually much closer to ways humans fail, like people bullshit all the time (is partially culturally mediated if you don't experience this try going to southern europe people will bullshit to safe face instead of giving you bad directions or whatever lol) => there is a lot of convergence there 

when I'm talking above about non-verbal stuff: there's this story by boyd varty where he's about to get executed by some gangsters and he changes his energy and they're so touched they spare him. that kind of thing could possibly work on bundy and I think humans on some level have some sort of alignment built in by being human and he had that module disabled and it might be possible to reactivate

also just TBC I absolutely believe you can have misaligned AI, just like misaligned humans, you don't need orthogonality for that

ok like this is rambly and I lost context, basically bundy is still a biological human acting on human imperatives and while he's "unaligned" locally he's globally still aligned biologically (kinda) such that there isn't anywhere near real orthogonality (bc the whole intelligence of a human should be considered instead of the sliver that's concious word conciousness or whatever)

man this is a mess I hope it makes sense lol

text:
I disagree with this. I agree that "ideas whose time have come" are a thing, but also the space of ideas is overwhelmingly large and there are many ideas whose time could have come who aren't instanciated. affordance constraints are real, some things would not happen without pepole explicitly making them happen and this can be very scary, e.g. columbine and school shootings. those were possible before but hadn't been done and they apparently purposely wanted to channel this into existence in some sense (never double checked this there is apparently some journal of theirs etc...). 

a different example: I don't think we'd have had apple or the iphone without steve jobs even though computation and mobile computation per se maybe are at the level of inevitability, the specifics of the affordance landscape matter an awful lot.
